event: Paleo-Indian hunter-gatherers inhabit central Mexico, leaving remains such as Tepexpan Man near the Basin of Mexico.
Early peoples in the Tehuacan Valley begin the domestication of maize, a foundation of Mesoamerican civilization.
The Olmec civilization, Mesoamerica's earliest major culture, emerges along the Gulf Coast at sites like San Lorenzo.
The Olmec center of La Venta flourishes, producing colossal stone heads and early calendrical and writing systems.
Monte Alban is founded in the Oaxaca Valley as the capital of the Zapotec civilization.
The great city of Teotihuacan rises in the Valley of Mexico, growing into one of the largest cities in the ancient world.
The Classic Maya civilization enters its peak in the Yucatan and southern lowlands, building cities like Palenque and Calakmul.
Teotihuacan reaches its height with over 100,000 inhabitants before its later decline and partial destruction.
The Classic Maya collapse leads to the abandonment of many southern lowland cities.
The Toltec civilization establishes its capital at Tula, becoming a dominant power in central Mexico.
The Mexica (Aztecs) found Tenochtitlan on an island in Lake Texcoco, per tradition where an eagle perched on a cactus.
The Aztec Triple Alliance of Tenochtitlan, Texcoco, and Tlacopan forms, building a vast tributary empire.
Moctezuma II becomes emperor, ruling the Aztec Empire at its territorial height.
Hernan Cortes lands on the Gulf Coast, founds Veracruz, and marches inland to Tenochtitlan.
The Spanish and indigenous allies conquer Tenochtitlan, ending the Aztec Empire and founding the colony of New Spain.
The reported apparitions of the Virgin of Guadalupe at Tepeyac become a central symbol of Mexican Catholicism and identity.
The Viceroyalty of New Spain is formally established with Antonio de Mendoza as the first viceroy.
Major silver deposits are discovered at Zacatecas, making mining the engine of New Spain's colonial economy.
The Royal and Pontifical University of Mexico is founded, among the first universities in the Americas.
Father Miguel Hidalgo issues the Grito de Dolores, launching the Mexican War of Independence.
Mexico achieves independence from Spain with the Treaty of Cordoba and the Plan of Iguala.
Mexico adopts a federal constitution, establishing the first Mexican Republic.
Texas declares independence from Mexico after the battles of the Alamo and San Jacinto.
The Mexican-American War begins following U.S. annexation of Texas and border disputes.
The Treaty of Guadalupe Hidalgo ends the war, ceding roughly half of Mexico's territory to the United States.
A liberal constitution enshrining individual rights and limiting Church power is promulgated, sparking the Reform War.
Mexican forces defeat the French army at the Battle of Puebla on May 5, commemorated as Cinco de Mayo.
France installs Maximilian of Habsburg as Emperor of Mexico during the French intervention.
Republican forces under Benito Juarez defeat and execute Maximilian, restoring the republic.
Porfirio Diaz seizes power, beginning the Porfiriato, a decades-long dictatorship of modernization and inequality.
The Mexican Revolution erupts as Francisco Madero challenges Diaz's rule.
A new constitution is enacted, introducing land reform, labor rights, and anticlerical and resource-nationalist provisions.
Alvaro Obregon's rise marks the end of the most violent phase of the revolution after roughly a million deaths.
The Cristero War breaks out as Catholics rebel against the government's enforcement of anticlerical laws.
The forerunner of the Institutional Revolutionary Party (PRI) is founded, beginning decades of one-party dominance.
President Lazaro Cardenas begins sweeping land redistribution and social reforms.
Cardenas nationalizes the oil industry, creating the state company Pemex.
Mexico becomes a founding member of the United Nations.
Women gain the right to vote in national elections.
Mexico City hosts the Olympic Games shortly after the Tlatelolco massacre of student protesters.
A devastating earthquake strikes Mexico City, killing thousands and exposing government weaknesses.
NAFTA takes effect and the Zapatista uprising erupts in Chiapas on the same day, January 1.
Vicente Fox of the PAN wins the presidency, ending 71 years of continuous PRI rule.
President Felipe Calderon launches a militarized war on drug cartels, triggering years of violence.
A powerful earthquake again strikes central Mexico and Mexico City, killing hundreds.
Andres Manuel Lopez Obrador wins a landslide presidential victory, shifting power to the leftist Morena party.
Claudia Sheinbaum is elected as Mexico's first woman president.
